name: Fushimi Inari-taisha
description: Ten thousand vermilion torii gates wind up the forested slopes of Mount Inari. The full hike to the summit takes about two hours and passes dozens of sub-shrines with panoramic city views.

name: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
description: Japan's most photographed building — a three-story Zen temple sheathed in gold leaf, perfectly mirrored in the still waters of the surrounding Kyoko-chi pond.

name: Kiyomizu-dera Temple
description: A UNESCO World Heritage temple perched on a hillside above eastern Kyoto. Its wooden stage juts 13 metres over the treetops, offering sweeping views of the city and Kyoto Tower.

name: Gion District
description: Kyoto's most famous geisha district — preserved machiya townhouses, stone-paved Hanamikoji-dori, lantern-lit tea houses, and the chance to spot a geiko or maiko at dusk.

name: Arashiyama Bamboo Grove
description: A corridor of towering bamboo stalks lines the path from Tenryu-ji to Nonomiya Shrine. Best visited at opening time to experience the eerie rustling in near-solitude.

name: Nijo Castle
description: The 17th-century Tokugawa shogunate palace, famed for 'nightingale floors' that squeak to alert against intruders. Lavish painted sliding screens and a moat-encircled garden.

name: Nishiki Market
description: A narrow 400-year-old covered arcade with 100+ stalls selling Kyoto pickles, fresh tofu, grilled skewers, matcha sweets, and every regional delicacy the city is known for.

name: Philosopher's Path
description: A stone-paved canal-side walkway linking Ginkaku-ji to Nanzen-ji, lined with hundreds of cherry trees and quiet cafés. Named after philosopher Nishida Kitaro's daily contemplative walks.

name: Ryoan-ji Rock Garden
description: Home to Japan's most famous karesansui garden — 15 stones arranged in white gravel so that only 14 are ever visible from any single viewpoint. A UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Ginkaku-ji (Silver Pavilion)
description: Despite its name, the pavilion was never covered in silver. The real draw is the Higashiyama-jishi sand cone and wave-raked dry garden of this supremely restrained Zen complex.

name: Tenryu-ji Garden
description: A masterpiece of Zen garden design by Musō Soseki, with a pond that mirrors the Arashiyama mountains. The 14th-century sōgen garden is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Sanjusangendo
description: Japan's longest wooden structure stretches 120m and houses 1,001 gilded Kannon statues — each with 11 heads and 40 arms — flanking a singular seated deity in solemn rows.

name: Pontocho Alley
description: A narrow stone-paved alley running between the Kamo River and Kiyamachi-dori, lined with kaiseki restaurants, yakitori bars, and intimate cocktail lounges.

name: Tofuku-ji Temple
description: One of Kyoto's great Rinzai Zen monasteries — famous for Shigemori Mirei's modernist checkerboard stone-and-moss gardens and the largest sanmon gate of any Japanese Zen temple.

name: Kyoto National Museum
description: Founded in 1897, the museum holds over 12,500 artifacts — Buddhist sculpture, ink paintings, lacquerware, and screens — sourced from Kyoto's greatest temples and shrines.

name: Daitoku-ji Temple Complex
description: A sprawling Rinzai Zen complex of 24 sub-temples, many with exquisite private gardens. Historically central to the tea ceremony — Sen no Rikyu had his own sub-temple here.

name: Kyoto Imperial Palace Park
description: Sprawling grounds surrounding the former imperial residence, now a public park with cherry blossoms, gravel paths, and free guided palace tours. A tranquil oasis in central Kyoto.

name: Fushimi Sake District
description: One of Japan's most celebrated sake-brewing regions. Centuries-old kura line the Fushimi Momoyama canal — most offer tours and tasting sessions of their signature junmai.

name: Maruyama Park
description: Kyoto's most popular public park, centred on a magnificent weeping cherry tree. Street food stalls line the paths and the park opens directly into Yasaka Shrine.

name: Nanzen-ji Temple & Aqueduct
description: A grand Rinzai Zen head temple famous for its soaring sanmon gate and a striking 19th-century brick aqueduct cutting through the grounds — one of Kyoto's most photogenic contrasts.
